title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several routine items and made key decisions including awarding a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, adopting a resolution supporting state transportation funding, establishing $100/day fines for illegal sewer discharges, and approving construction agreements for multiple road projects. The committee also received updates on significant grant funding totaling over $9 million in recent years.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 water and sewer permits
Water and sewer connection agreement for Radkey Avenue
Construction service proposals for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally a 2029 project now likely moving to 2028. The grant covers over 50% of the approximately $1.5 million project. Michael noted the village has secured over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Updates were also provided on Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading, Schofield Avenue repairs with HOS Construction, and Fuller Street work with Melvin. The Bladel special assessment meeting was tentatively scheduled for Thursday, May 28th at 6:00 PM.
Motion to approve made, seconded, and passed unanimously with no discussion.

Staff reported on spring activities including removal of road limits on April 29th, mailbox and sign repairs, dewatering operations along Weston Avenue tributary for approximately one week, curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Storm damage from East Nick was addressed with tree removal. Culvert failures on Jones Street required installation of additional 18-inch culverts. Equipment maintenance updates included changeover from winter to summer operations and various vehicle servicing.

Josh reported on water service repairs at Caribou Coffee, well maintenance, and private well sampling. On the sanitary side, lift station pump maintenance was significant due to high groundwater. A major concern was identified with clear water infiltration - April showed 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated, indicating approximately 15 million gallons of clear water from infiltration or illegal sump pump discharges. Staff discovered residents illegally connecting sump pumps to sanitary sewers. The committee discussed upcoming INI study by In-Pipe Technologies to identify problem areas.

Michael presented the annual report showing operating net income of approximately $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was reported at 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with 6.7% infrastructure placed in service including wells 7 and 8. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers total: 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in other areas. Cash reserves total $6.8 million. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution supports long-term transportation funding solutions. Grant funding helps one-time costs but recurring annual payments for street maintenance and snow plowing remain underfunded. The village has decreased public works budgets recently. Wisconsin remains affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership costs. Motion to recommend village board adoption passed unanimously.

Michael presented quotes for crushing recycled concrete and asphalt at the Ryan Street site. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. Analysis showed the village's cost of $17.11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau paying $28/cubic yard, resulting in approximately $130,000 savings across three 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Staff sought guidance on enforcement of illegal sump pump discharges to sanitary sewers. Current code allows fines of $100-$1,000 per day per violation. Committee discussed distinguishing between unknowing violations (first-time notice with correction period) versus willful violations. Chair Zagami suggested waiting for In-Pipe Technologies to complete their analysis, then sending notices to identified violators with 30-60 day correction periods. Committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines starting at $100/day for first offenses. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael explained a vacant lot at 2230 Radkey Avenue needs utility service for a planned duplex. Current mains dead-end at Radkey Avenue. Rather than requiring the developer to install new mains through Outlot 1, staff proposed allowing temporary long lateral connections with agreement to pay assessments when Radkey Avenue is reconstructed. Committee added language specifying reconstruction "no later than 2029." Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

The village will reimburse Rothschild for water and sewer work as part of Military Road reconstruction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Weston's utility costs total $94,860 ($6,156 sanitary sewer, $88,704 water main). Water main must be relocated due to storm sewer conflicts. Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

Michael presented proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelnik roundabout project: MSA at $37,315 (recommended) vs Clark Dietz at $48,910. For Bladel Avenue: MSA at $70,400 vs Clark Dietz at $73,730. Staff recommended Clark Dietz for Bladel despite slightly higher cost because MSA proposed using a summer intern while Clark Dietz would use full-time experienced staff. Motion to approve MSA for roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Village board to adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with $100/day fine structure
Approve water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkey Avenue with reconstruction deadline of 2029
Approve intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction ($94,860 Weston share)
Award construction services: MSA for Alderson/Jelnik project, Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue project
Schedule Bladel special assessment meeting for Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration impact at next meeting
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs starting Wednesday/Thursday

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its first meeting after reorganization, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Work product transmittals, educational presentations and reports
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Remarks from staff and commission members

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated and elected as Vice Chair with Jackson providing the second. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary. All elections passed unanimously with no opposition.
The minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill from 3308 Green Pastures Lane presented the grant request. He reported approximately 11,000-12,000 attendees last year and announced that a competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making Taste and Glow the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commissioner Hodal questioned room tracking, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year when they received $5,000. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with Jackson seconding. Motion passed unanimously. The commission requested better hotel room tracking data for future reports.

Renee reported attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar with Jamie. She met the candidates and noted that Anastasia, who sat at their table, won the title. This was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s. The candidates were from various Wisconsin universities and were impressed with Marathon County's community.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow organizers to provide accurate hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved a site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Drive, and adopted a salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. Both items passed unanimously with staff conditions.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility site plan
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for project number 20260116 at 3600 Community Center Drive. Jen explained the building meets all B2 zoning requirements and landscaping standards. Police Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to accommodate a gas pipeline easement, with the parking lot now positioned over the 36-inch deep gas line. The building has capacity for 50-60 officers versus current staffing of 40 officers and 4 clerical staff. An alternate storage shed location was discussed, with commissioners suggesting it be placed further north to accommodate future expansion. Motion passed unanimously to recommend approval to the board of trustees subject to staff conditions 2-5.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for Wellhead Protection District
discussions.body: Staff proposed a twice-annual inspection program for salt storage facilities in wellhead protection areas, including a warm weather inspection focused on structural integrity and a winter operational inspection. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards was developed. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg Road has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process. Commission discussed reaching out to that facility for compliance. Motion passed unanimously to approve the program with reporting back to both the Plan Commission and Public Works committee.

discussions.item: Planning and development department monthly update
discussions.body: Jen announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission-Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Commissioner asked about potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing. Staff indicated Ehlers has prepared a presentation on residential TIF qualifications that could be shared with the commission or incorporated into the comprehensive plan process.

publicComment: Jim Penson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston spoke via video call. He urged the commission to exercise common sense on landscaping requirements for the police department project to avoid excessive taxpayer costs similar to the Green Heck Turner building. He also requested annual reporting on salt shed inspections in wellhead protection zones, noting some zoning complaints have gone unresolved for years.
actionItems: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department site plan recommended for approval to Board of Trustees subject to staff conditions
Salt storage shed inspection program approved for implementation with reporting to Plan Commission and Public Works
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility on Sternberg Road regarding wellhead protection compliance
Joint Plan Commission-Village Board steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Staff to send email and calendar invite for June 1st steering committee meeting
Next regular meeting scheduled for Monday, June 8, 2026 at 6:00 p.m.

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new board members, featuring presentations from the Health Department, Social Services, Veterans Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center. No formal votes were taken beyond approving meeting minutes; the meeting focused on educating committee members about departmental missions, budgets, and key programs.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call (noting remote participation)
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operations functions required by statute - discussion of agenda structure
Educational presentations overview and committee ordinance review

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavalli moved to approve the minutes, Vice Chair Rosenberg seconded. The motion passed unanimously with voice vote.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease, promoting health, and protecting from environmental hazards. She highlighted the department's 37.6 FTE staff across four divisions, over 1,200 licenses overseen, public health vending machines stocked with free tick kits and harm reduction supplies, the Nurse Family Partnership program now graduating its first two-year-olds, and a new FTE position for homeless system response. About half of their $5 million budget comes from tax levy, with significant funding from intergovernmental grants and public charges for services.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who was unavailable. The department has nearly 140 FTE and a budget over $22 million with about $7 million in tax levy. He highlighted the successful Family Keys pilot program addressing housing instability for child welfare and youth justice families, noting many participants had never had bank accounts or been on a formal lease. He mentioned potential restructuring requests coming for increased staffing needs in children's long-term support and child support units.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo reported the office increased from 2.75 to 3.0 FTE and interactions jumped from 5,900 in 2024 to 8,149 in 2025. He noted $44 million in annual VA benefits comes into the county, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Key initiatives include working with surviving spouses on dependency and indemnity compensation benefits (up to $2,400/month tax-free), partnering with Center for Veterans Issues for housing stability, and addressing veteran suicide prevention after 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans. Supervisor Kremes asked about regional coordination; Galindo explained state statute requires each county to have its own VSO but they collaborate across county lines.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the ADRC serves four counties (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) as a one-stop shop for aging and disability services. The organization has about 60 FTE plus 385-400 volunteers, with only 11% of budget from county levy. He highlighted significant financial challenges with nutrition/Meals on Wheels program, noting food costs increased 40-45% over four years while federal Older Americans Act funding remains flat at 2024 levels. The organization hadn't requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024, demonstrating the regional model's efficiency.

Chair Boots noted the county recently finished the comprehensive plan and will be developing a new strategic plan targeting completion by December. He indicated future meetings will track progress on strategic plan objectives relevant to this committee.

actionItems: April 1st meeting minutes approved
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before committee
ADRC Executive Director to present annual report with 2025 data to full county board
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with items for future agendas
Next meeting scheduled for Wednesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (corrected from initially stated May 29th)

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees for new residential development, passed several ordinance amendments including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bicycle regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with relocating the farmers market to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call, and public comments
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fee amendments (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Approval of meeting minutes from April 13 and April 21, 2026
Acknowledgment of reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda including vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, standing committees, nuisances, and e-bicycles
Committee appointments and secondhand dealer license approval
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Park and Recreation Impact Fee Amendments
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed fee increases from $300 to $761 for single family units, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and to $495 for multifamily units, consistent with the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment questioning the justification for the numbers, stating the village lacks solid data on actual park maintenance costs. Trustee Barb Ermeling supported the fee increase, noting population growth requires more park space. Ordinance 26-010 was approved unanimously on motion by Lope Sorrell, seconded by Zagami. Resolution 2026-015 was also approved unanimously on motion by Ermeling, seconded by Clark.

Chief Finkey explained this ordinance establishes a regional agreement with Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Clover Belt, and Riverside where one fire inspection by any participating department is valid for all communities. A sticker system indicates inspection completion, eliminating redundant inspections for food trucks operating across multiple municipalities. Approved unanimously on motion by Lope Sorrell, seconded by Zagami.

The board discussed the distinction between e-bikes and e-motos, with trustees noting e-motos can reach 52 mph and are essentially like motorcycles. The ordinance amends chapter 82 regarding traffic and bicycles. Approved unanimously on motion by Zagami, seconded by Clark.

Significant debate occurred over the policy language. Trustee Beck questioned whether the policy aligned with what was voted on at the previous meeting, noting the draft still contained language about health, safety, and welfare justifications that the board had intended to remove. After discussion, the board voted to strike sentences requiring members to justify remote attendance with specific reasons, keeping the policy broad with requirements for 12-hour advance notice, camera on, and no participation in closed sessions. The amendment passed with two opposed (Ermeling and Lope Sorrell), and the amended policy passed with two opposed (same trustees).

The president appointed Golden Mua and Stephanie Daniels to the CLIPS committee. Golden Mua was noted as potentially joining Finance committee later after getting experience. Approved unanimously on motion by Ermeling, seconded by Lope Sorrell. The board also agreed to include committee applicant resumes in future board packets.

discussions.item: Secondhand Article and Jewelry Dealer License
discussions.body: Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street applied for secondhand dealer licenses. Chief Hunt explained these licenses protect against stolen merchandise through a reporting system accessible to police. The license was approved with an amendment requiring satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit, as the building is still under construction. Approved unanimously.

The board discussed challenges with the farmers market's relocation to Kennedy Park after losing their previous location on Camp Phillips and Scoffield Avenue when the property owner decided to develop the land. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about cars parking on grass along Alderson Street. Discussion noted the market has grown from 25 to 53 vendor spots. Renee explained the market operates at a surplus through vendor fees and grants for programs like double up food bucks. Mock Miller Park was mentioned as a possible alternative but concerns were raised about low visibility and traffic. No action was taken; discussion only.

Public Works Director Michael Dolan reported on speed study data showing the 85th percentile speed was 45 mph, with most traffic traveling between 44-50 mph. The DOT engineering study recommended 35 mph, but the village is seeking to justify 40-45 mph. A trustee noted the speed limit issue has been unresolved for over two months, making current signage inconsistent with ordinance. Staff will bring the matter back to a future board meeting.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52 Street spoke during general public comment criticizing the hiring of outside engineering consultants for $108,000 when an engineering technician position remains unfilled, questioning administrator accountability. He also criticized the appointment of Lisa Beck to the Safer board before official board action, calling it 'inappropriate government business taking action outside of this boardroom.' He requested a detailed report on Kennedy Park funding timeline. During the park impact fee public hearing, Pitel again spoke questioning whether the fee increase was justified without solid data on actual park maintenance costs.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased effective with passage of Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015
Mobile food vendor regional inspection agreement implemented via Ordinance 26-008
E-bicycle regulations updated via Ordinance 26-012
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements
Golden Mua and Stephanie Daniels appointed to CLIPS committee
Secondhand dealer license approved for Scoffield Coin and Hobby pending background check and occupancy permit
Staff to prepare intergovernmental service agreement with Town of Ringle for building inspection assistance
Comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1 at 6:00 PM
Speed limit issue for Weston Avenue to return to board at future meeting
Committee applicant resumes to be included in future board packets

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board held an adjourned organizational meeting where committee appointments were approved, two at-large members were elected to the Executive Committee, and a resolution was unanimously passed to reduce landfill tipping fees for debris from the recent severe weather disaster affecting Ringle and other communities. The fee reduction from $65 to approximately $36.94 per ton will remain in effect through June 17th, with the state and town of Ringle waiving their portions of the fee.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Roll call (technical difficulties with meeting manager system)
Committee appointments - Chair remarks and goals
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for disaster debris

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs outlined goals for committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, including increasing diversity of perspectives, ensuring leadership development for newer supervisors (34% are in first or second term), and honoring supervisor preferences. The motion to approve both county board chair and administrator appointments was made by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous, with Supervisor Mask unable to vote due to technical issues.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained her nomination of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for the county's youngest and oldest residents. Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two of the three candidates. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.

Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for disaster debris from $65 to approximately $36.94 per ton. The state is waiving $13.10 and the town of Ringle is waiving $2.33. Public comment was allowed by special dispensation for Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, both requesting Marathon County wave its fees entirely. Toasty noted 44 homes affected and requested a six-month waiver. Multiple supervisors asked clarifying questions about fee structures, timelines, and future policy. The board first voted unanimously to suspend rules to consider the resolution, then passed Resolution 26-26 unanimously.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty argued that Marathon County should waive fees entirely as the state, DNR, and Ringle have done, noting that private businesses have donated up to $40,000 in cleanup efforts and residents are facing significant out-of-pocket expenses. She requested a six-month fee waiver. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of affected homeowners, noting some of the approximately 44 damaged homes are still awaiting insurance adjusters and requested more time for the fee waiver.
actionItems: Resolution 26-26 approved: Landfill tipping fees reduced to approximately $36.94 per ton for certified disaster debris through June 17, 2025
Committee appointments approved for 2025 term
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to Executive Committee at-large positions
Administrator Leonard to explore partnership with Community Foundation for reimbursing uninsured disposal costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop formal policy on disaster-related fee reductions within six months
Resolution 24-26 approved: Out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26 approved: Social services budget amendment

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions unanimously and voted to forward a modified fee waiver proposal to the full county board that would only waive reconnect fees for septic systems damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado, rather than the broader fee waiver originally proposed by staff. The committee also received educational presentations from Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing - Rezoning petition for James Offer and David Tract (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential)
Public hearing - Rezoning petition for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz (conservancy recreation to general agriculture)
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentations - Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentations - Parks, Recreation and Forestry overview
Educational presentations - Conservation Planning and Zoning overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Rezoning - James Offer and David Tract
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential in the town of Green Valley. The town board recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and the town's approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

The petition sought to rezone from conservancy recreation to general agriculture in McMillan township. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature of the property; staff explained it currently has a home and horse riding stable, and rezoning would make the structures conforming uses. No natural areas would be lost. Supervisor Sefelt moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

Staff proposed waiving county zoning and septic fees for properties damaged by the April 17 tornado, affecting up to 149 parcels. Significant committee debate ensued. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Sefelt expressed concern about unanticipated expenses and potential levy impacts. Supervisor Drebeck raised fairness concerns about individual tornado victims not receiving similar treatment. After discussion, Supervisor Drebeck amended the motion to limit fee waivers only to reconnect fees ($160) for existing septic systems, excluding new system permits and other fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor Kurt and passed. The amended resolution was then approved to forward to the county board, though not unanimously.

Director Dave Hagen Busher provided an overview covering landfill operations, the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy, lithium-ion battery fire challenges, leachate treatment requirements by 2032, and upcoming capital projects including $6 million for cell construction.

Tom Lovelin, county forest administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie. He noted this is his last committee meeting before retiring June 1 after 30 years. The department operates 17 county parks, 30,700 acres of county forest, and provides services to the City of Wausau. Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million annually for the local economy from out-of-town visitors.

actionItems: Forward amended resolution to county board waiving only septic reconnect fees for tornado-damaged properties
Forward James Offer/David Tract rezoning to county board for approval
Forward Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning to county board for approval
Staff to track costs and requests related to tornado fee waivers for future policy development
Conservation Planning and Zoning to schedule summer educational meeting on conservation topics

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Parks and Recreation Committee received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and construction timelines, deferred discussion on dogs at the aquatic center to next month, and approved signage for the Eau Claire River kayak launches. Public comment included opposition to allowing dogs at the pool and criticism of Kennedy Park financial reporting transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on the Eau Claire River Trail discussion

discussions.body: Minutes were approved with a motion and second. No discussion occurred. Motion carried unanimously with all present voting in favor.

Director Sean Oster Bring presented a timeline of the Kennedy Park master plan process from its approval in November 2023 through the May 9th ribbon cutting. Committee member Scott asked about village capital dollars, and Oster Bring clarified that infrastructure funds were put on hold pending the master plan project. The Friends of Kennedy Park currently have approximately $84-85,000 at the community foundation toward the $117,000 needed for field lights, and plan to make a public funding announcement at their June tournament. Chair emphasized that Friends of Kennedy Park deserve grace as volunteers and encouraged community members to get involved rather than criticize.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff presented photos showing the location of trees removed from the river and the installation of wood mats over gas pipelines to protect them during equipment access. Dan explained the mats were placed across two pipeline locations and the village retained extra mats that may be used on future trail projects.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: Discussion was deferred to next month because Committee Chair Katrina was absent due to an unforeseen issue. The chair noted that Katrina had requested this item and should have the opportunity to speak on it. The resident who spoke during public comment against dogs at the pool was informed his objection was noted and he was welcome to return next month.

discussions.item: Graffiti on Eau Claire River Trail
discussions.body: Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete structures due to limited equipment access on the trail. Memorial benches weigh 2,000 pounds each and cannot easily be removed for cleaning. Committee discussed applying anti-vandalism coatings or sealers to make cleaning easier, and the possibility of volunteer work days similar to how original installations were done with director team building days. No formal action taken; staff will explore cleaning options.

Dan from Marathon County Parks presented version 7 of proposed signage for Eau Claire River kayak launches. Signs will be installed at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks landings within the Village of Weston, plus locations in City of Schofield. Signs include maps, QR codes for river gauge information, and location-specific history sections. The Highway J launch may be closed for the season due to bridge replacement. Motion to approve village installation of the signs passed unanimously.

Staff requested committee interest in discussing food allergy awareness signage at playgrounds, noting an organization offered to provide signs free of charge. Committee agreed to bring the topic back for future discussion and education. Staff also announced the Mock Miller master plan process will begin with a kickoff meeting with Rettler, and the final bike and pedestrian plan open house is June 11th from 5-7pm.

publicComment: Two speakers offered public comment. A resident at 6504 [inaudible street] spoke against allowing dogs at the public pool, stating animals should not be in public pools and belong in rivers and lakes. Jim Pensan (appearing online under the name 'Vern M') at 5002 [inaudible] Street Weston criticized the 4pm meeting time as discouraging public participation, questioned the Kennedy Park funding report showing exactly $8,700 in receipts and expenditures as 'too coincidental,' asked about the location of a $10,000 grant and glow event fundraising money, and accused the Friends of Kennedy Park of being misleading about finances.
actionItems: Approved minutes from April 27, 2026 meeting
Deferred dog day at aquatic center discussion to June meeting
Approved installation of Eau Claire River kayak launch signs at Yellow Banks, Ross Avenue, and Highway J locations
Staff to explore graffiti removal options and anti-vandalism coatings for trail structures
Food allergy awareness signage at playgrounds to be added to future agenda
Mock Miller master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged April financial reports. The meeting featured an educational presentation by Municipal Clerk Pam on the clerk's office budget and operations, covering elections, licensing, tax collection, and the quarterly newsletter. Committee member Evan announced he is stepping down as this was his last meeting.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
Work product transmittals - April budget status report
TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports for April
Old business, new business, staff remarks

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. No discussion. Motion carried unanimously with voice vote.

discussions.item: TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports
discussions.body: A committee member asked about TIF closure timeline. Staff explained they are anticipating October/November for final closure, with bonds due October 1st. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, likely this summer. Staff confirmed the numbers look good and they will be able to pay off the debt.
Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF detail reports, and legal details report made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented comprehensive overview of the clerk's office operations. The office has reduced from four full-time staff to two full-time and one part-time employee. She noted 84% of tax collection ($22 million) is handled through walk-ins at the clerk's office. Pet licensing was brought back in-house in 2025 after 12 years with third-party Pet Data, saving approximately $5,495 annually. Committee discussed the pet license fee increase from $10 to $20, which was driven by Marathon County raising fees to cover their $100,000 annual payment to the Humane Society. The $175 per animal cost for non-dog strays was questioned, including $175 charged for picking up mice. Newsletter costs of approximately $30,000 annually for printing and postage were discussed, with staff noting previous attempts to eliminate mailing were reversed due to resident complaints.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well for three weeks. Saturdays have been successful with full vendor capacity including food trucks, coffee vendors, craft people, and greenhouse vendors. Tuesdays have been challenging due to weather. Committee members raised concerns about parking on Alderson and Newport streets. Staff clarified parking is only allowed on the west side of Alderson, with no parking restrictions on Newport.

Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though staying in the metro area. This was his last meeting. Committee thanked him for his service.

actionItems: CDA to pass resolution this summer to call TIF bonds due October 1st
Staff to coordinate with Ellers and notify associate trust regarding bond calling
Recruit new committee members to fill open spots on this and other committees
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to the nuisance ordinance, recommended a new e-bike ordinance with restrictions on emotos, and authorized a raze order for a dilapidated structure at 4104 Shore Avenue. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations and a crosswalk request, deferring both for further staff work.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment

discussions.item: Code enforcement, Fire/EMS, and Police department reports
discussions.body: Staff presented departmental reports. Roman noted 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the code enforcement roll after the property sold at auction and the new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), issued 281 citations, and conducted 410 traffic stops. The department has hired its 40th officer. Motion to acknowledge reports passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments to the nuisance ordinance, including changes to tree limb height requirements over streets and walkways (14 and 10 feet were switched per previous discussion). Olsen made a motion to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

The committee discussed a model e-bike ordinance for multiple municipalities. Chief Hunt strongly recommended prohibiting emotos (electric dirt bikes) on roadways and sidewalks due to safety concerns, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Discussion included whether to exempt children's electric scooters. The committee decided to add language prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property. Olsen motioned to recommend approval with the emoto prohibition added, seconded by Sherwanka. Motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Overnight parking regulations October 31st to May 1st
discussions.body: Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour overnight parking window (4am-4pm) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities (typically 5 hours or less). Public Works Director Michael suggested either eliminating the overnight restriction entirely and signing specific problem areas, or matching Rothschild's approach. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight restrictions for enforcement purposes. The committee decided to defer to July, asking Michael to identify the 6-8 specific problem areas that need signage.

discussions.item: Crosswalk request on Alderson Street between Mount Olive Church and DC Everest
discussions.body: The committee discussed a request for a crosswalk where church members cross from street parking to Mount Olive Church. A basic crosswalk would cost approximately $2,000-$3,000; a lighted system would cost $25,000-$30,000. The committee was reluctant to set precedent of paying for crosswalks on request. Olsen motioned to recommend approval to the board if Mount Olive agrees to cover the installation cost, seconded by Sherwanka. Motion passed unanimously. Michael will contact the church.

discussions.item: Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment
discussions.body: Fire Marshal Marty proposed joining a regional fire inspection program with Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Weston. Food trucks inspected by any participating department receive a sticker valid for all jurisdictions. Chief noted one recent inspection failed due to improper LP gas piping and unsecured propane containers. Wausau opted out to do their own inspections. Sherwanka motioned to approve Ordinance 26-8, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: a duplex at 5915 Delicoski Street needing separate addresses, out-of-sequence addresses on Ross Avenue requiring renumbering, and a new duplex on Radkey Avenue. Chief Hunt also identified Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski as having nine separate addresses for one eight-unit structure. Motion to recommend address corrections passed unanimously.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings on an abandoned structure from 2010-2011. A previous fire training burn was cancelled because the structure was too deteriorated for safe training. The property owners are current on taxes and have agreed to the demolition, with costs to be added to their tax roll. Olsen motioned to recommend authorization, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding several agenda items. He expressed concern that the new farmers market location at the park will destroy grassy areas by allowing vehicle parking on grass, and questioned whether this violates village code. He noted ADA-compliant handicap parking should be on firm surfaces rather than grass. He questioned the origin of the new fire inspection requirement for mobile food vendors. He also raised concerns about code enforcement cases, noting some have been pending for years with no visible progress, and requested periodic status updates to the committee.
actionItems: Recommend approval of Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to Board of Trustees
Recommend approval of e-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property to Board of Trustees
Michael to identify 6-8 specific areas needing overnight parking signage for July meeting
Michael to contact Mount Olive Church regarding cost-sharing for crosswalk installation
Recommend approval of Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections to Board of Trustees
Recommend approval of E911 address corrections to Board of Trustees
Recommend authorization to begin raze proceedings at 4104 Shore Avenue to Board of Trustees
Staff to investigate property concerns at Sternberg and South Timber intersection

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation

Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
